question:
necessarily distinct) decimal digits. The number $e_{1}e_{2}\dots
e_{9}$ is such that each of the nine 9-digit numbers formed by
replacing just one of the digits $d_{i}$ is $d_{1}d_{2}\dots d_{9}$
by the corresponding digit $e_{i}$ ($1 \leq i \leq 9$) is divisible
by 7. The number $f_{1}f_{2}\dots f_{9}$ is related to
$e_{1}e_{2}\dots e_{9}$ is the same way: that is, each of the nine
numbers formed by replacing one of the $e_{i}$ by the corresponding
$f_{i}$ is divisible by 7. Show that, for each $i$, $d_{i}-f_{i}$ is
divisible by 7. [For example, if $d_{1}d_{2}\dots d_{9} = 199501996$,
then $e_{6}$ may be 2 or 9, since $199502996$ and $199509996$ are
multiples of 7.]
solution:
Let $D$ and $E$ be the numbers $d_{1}\dots d_{9}$ and $e_{1}\dots
e_{9}$, respectively. We are given that $(e_{i} - d_{i})10^{9-i} + D
\equiv 0 \pmod 7$ and $(f_{i} - e_{i})10^{9-i} + E \equiv 0 \pmod 7$
for $i=1, \dots, 9$. Sum the first relation over $i=1,\dots,9$ and we
get $E - D + 9D \equiv 0 \pmod 7$, or $E + D \equiv 0 \pmod 7$. Now
add the first and second relations for any particular value of $i$
and we get $(f_{i} - d_{i})10^{9-i} + E + D \equiv 0 \pmod 7$. But we
know $E+D$ is divisible by 7, and 10 is coprime to 7, so $d_{i} -
f_{i} \equiv 0 \pmod 7$.

descriptive_long.question: necessarily distinct) decimal digits. The number $edigitoneedigittwo\dots edigitnine$ is such that each of the nine 9-digit numbers formed by replacing just one of the digits $ddigitvar$ is $ddigitoneddigittwo\dots ddigitnine$ by the corresponding digit $edigitvar$ ($1 \leq indexvar \leq 9$) is divisible by 7. The number $fdigitonefdigittwo\dots fdigitnine$ is related to $edigitoneedigittwo\dots edigitnine$ is the same way: that is, each of the nine numbers formed by replacing one of the $edigitvar$ by the corresponding $fdigitvar$ is divisible by 7. Show that, for each indexvar, $ddigitvar-fdigitvar$ is divisible by 7. [For example, if $ddigitoneddigittwo\dots ddigitnine = 199501996$, then $e_{6}$ may be 2 or 9, since $199502996$ and $199509996$ are multiples of 7.]
descriptive_long.solution: Let $dwholenum$ and $ewholenum$ be the numbers $ddigitoneddigittwo\dots ddigitnine$ and $edigitoneedigittwo\dots edigitnine$, respectively. We are given that $(edigitvar - ddigitvar)10^{9-indexvar} + dwholenum \equiv 0 \pmod 7$ and $(fdigitvar - edigitvar)10^{9-indexvar} + ewholenum \equiv 0 \pmod 7$ for $indexvar=1, \dots, 9$. Sum the first relation over $indexvar=1,\dots,9$ and we get $ewholenum - dwholenum + 9dwholenum \equiv 0 \pmod 7$, or $ewholenum + dwholenum \equiv 0 \pmod 7$. Now add the first and second relations for any particular value of $indexvar$ and we get $(fdigitvar - ddigitvar)10^{9-indexvar} + ewholenum + dwholenum \equiv 0 \pmod 7$. But we know $ewholenum+dwholenum$ is divisible by 7, and 10 is coprime to 7, so $ddigitvar - fdigitvar \equiv 0 \pmod 7$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: necessarily distinct) decimal digits. The number $gingerroot butterscot\dots kingfisher$ is such that each of the nine 9-digit numbers formed by replacing just one of the digits $paddleboat$ is $thumbtack raincloud\dots blackberry$ by the corresponding digit $aftershock$ ($1 \leq snowstorm \leq 9$) is divisible by 7. The number $peppermint carpetweed\dots dragonfly$ is related to $gingerroot butterscot\dots kingfisher$ is the same way: that is, each of the nine numbers formed by replacing one of the $aftershock$ by the corresponding $cheesecake$ is divisible by 7. Show that, for each $snowstorm$, $paddleboat-\,cheesecake$ is divisible by 7. [For example, if $thumbtack raincloud\dots blackberry = 199501996$, then $e_{6}$ may be 2 or 9, since $199502996$ and $199509996$ are multiples of 7.]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution: Let $mackerel$ and $partridge$ be the numbers $thumbtack\dots blackberry$ and $gingerroot\dots kingfisher$, respectively. We are given that $(aftershock - paddleboat)10^{9-snowstorm} + mackerel \equiv 0 \pmod 7$ and $(cheesecake - aftershock)10^{9-snowstorm} + partridge \equiv 0 \pmod 7$ for $snowstorm = 1, \dots, 9$. Sum the first relation over $snowstorm = 1,\dots,9$ and we get $partridge - mackerel + 9mackerel \equiv 0 \pmod 7$, or $partridge + mackerel \equiv 0 \pmod 7$. Now add the first and second relations for any particular value of $snowstorm$ and we get $(cheesecake - paddleboat)10^{9-snowstorm} + partridge + mackerel \equiv 0 \pmod 7$. But we know $partridge+mackerel$ is divisible by 7, and 10 is coprime to 7, so $paddleboat - cheesecake \equiv 0 \pmod 7$.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: necessarily distinct) decimal digits. The number $letteronelettertwo\dots letternine$ is such that each of the nine 9-digit numbers formed by replacing just one of the digits $blankindex$ is $blankoneblanktwo\dots blanknine$ by the corresponding digit $letterindex$ ($1 \leq stopindex \leq 9$) is divisible by 7. The number $spaceonespacetwo\dots spacenine$ is related to $letteronelettertwo\dots letternine$ is the same way: that is, each of the nine numbers formed by replacing one of the $letterindex$ by the corresponding $spaceindex$ is divisible by 7. Show that, for each $stopindex$, $blankindex-spaceindex$ is divisible by 7. [For example, if $blankoneblanktwo\dots blanknine = 199501996$, then $letter_{6}$ may be 2 or 9, since $199502996$ and $199509996$ are multiples of 7.]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution: Let $emptiness$ and $fullness$ be the numbers $blankone\dots blanknine$ and $letterone\dots letternine$, respectively. We are given that $(letterindex - blankindex)10^{9-stopindex} + emptiness \equiv 0 \pmod 7$ and $(spaceindex - letterindex)10^{9-stopindex} + fullness \equiv 0 \pmod 7$ for $stopindex=1, \dots, 9$. Sum the first relation over $stopindex=1,\dots,9$ and we get $fullness - emptiness + 9emptiness \equiv 0 \pmod 7$, or $fullness + emptiness \equiv 0 \pmod 7$. Now add the first and second relations for any particular value of $stopindex$ and we get $(spaceindex - blankindex)10^{9-stopindex} + fullness + emptiness \equiv 0 \pmod 7$. But we know $fullness+emptiness$ is divisible by 7, and 10 is coprime to 7, so $blankindex - spaceindex \equiv 0 \pmod 7$. Hence each $blankindex-spaceindex$ is divisible by 7.

garbled_string.question:
necessarily distinct) decimal digits. The number $qzxwvtnphjgrksla\dots
plmnorvw$ is such that each of the nine 9-digit numbers formed by
replacing just one of the digits $zxcvbnmq$ is $sdfghjklmnbvcxzq\dots lkjhgfdp$
by the corresponding digit $cbvtyxqp$ ($1 \leq zvbnmasd \leq 9$) is divisible
by 7. The number $rtyuioasgfdsaqwe\dots poiuytre$ is related to
$qzxwvtnphjgrksla\dots plmnorvw$ is the same way: that is, each of the nine
numbers formed by replacing one of the $cbvtyxqp$ by the corresponding
$wertyuio$ is divisible by 7. Show that, for each $zvbnmasd$, $zxcvbnmq-wertyuio$ is
divisible by 7. [For example, if $sdfghjklmnbvcxzq\dots lkjhgfdp = 199501996$,
then $e_{6}$ may be 2 or 9, since $199502996$ and $199509996$ are
multiples of 7.]
garbled_string.solution:
Let $asdfghjk$ and $qwertyui$ be the numbers $sdfghjkl\dots
lkjhgfdp$ and $qzxwvtnphjgrksla\dots plmnorvw$, respectively. We are given that $(cbvtyxqp - zxcvbnmq)10^{9-zvbnmasd} + asdfghjk 
\equiv 0 \pmod 7$ and $(wertyuio - cbvtyxqp)10^{9-zvbnmasd} + qwertyui \equiv 0 \pmod 7$
for $zvbnmasd=1, \dots, 9$. Sum the first relation over $zvbnmasd=1,\dots,9$ and we
get $qwertyui - asdfghjk + 9asdfghjk \equiv 0 \pmod 7$, or
$qwertyui + asdfghjk \equiv 0 \pmod 7$. Now add the first and second relations for any particular value of $zvbnmasd$
and we get $(wertyuio - zxcvbnmq)10^{9-zvbnmasd} + qwertyui + asdfghjk \equiv 0 \pmod 7$. But we
know $qwertyui+asdfghjk$ is divisible by 7, and 10 is coprime to 7, so $zxcvbnmq -
wertyuio \equiv 0 \pmod 7$.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
p:=11,\qquad b:=p+1=12,\qquad n:=p+2=13,\qquad 
k_i:=n-i\quad(1\le i\le n).
\]

For integers \(d_i,e_i,f_i\;(1\le i\le n)\) put  
\[
D=\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i\,b^{\,k_i},\qquad 
E=\sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i\,b^{\,k_i},\qquad 
F=\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i\,b^{\,k_i}.
\]

For every non-empty subset \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots ,n\}\) define the
single-step digit-replacement numbers  
\[
D[S\!\to\!E]\;:=\;
D+\sum_{i\in S}(e_i-d_i)\,b^{\,k_i},\qquad
E[i\!\to\!F]\;:=\;
E+(f_i-e_i)\,b^{\,k_i}\quad(1\le i\le n).
\]

Assume  

A)  For every non-empty \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots ,n\}\) with
   \(\lvert S\rvert\le 3\) one has  
   \[
      p^{\lvert S\rvert}\;\mid\; D[S\!\to\!E].
   \]

B)  For every index \(i\;(1\le i\le n)\) one has  
   \[
      p^{4}\;\mid\; E[i\!\to\!F].
   \]

Prove that, for every \(i\),  
\[
p^{2}\ \mid\ (d_i-f_i).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout the proof let \(v_p(\,\cdot\,)\) denote the
\(p\)-adic valuation.  
For every non-negative integer \(k\) we use the truncated binomial
expansion
\[
b^{k}=(1+p)^{k}=1+k\,p+\dfrac{k(k-1)}{2}\,p^{2}
             \pmod{p^{3}}. \tag{E}
\]

Introduce the differences  
\[
x_i:=e_i-d_i,\qquad y_i:=f_i-e_i\qquad(1\le i\le n).
\]

Part I - proving \(p^{2}\mid x_i\).  
-----------------------------------

STEP 1.  (Congruence modulo \(p\)).  
For the single-element set \(S=\{i\}\) condition (A) gives
\[
p\mid D+x_i b^{\,k_i}.
\]
Because \(b^{k_i}\equiv 1\pmod p\), we have
\[
D+x_i\equiv 0\pmod p\qquad(1\le i\le n).
\]
Hence the residues \(x_i\bmod p\) are all equal; write
\[
x_i\equiv c\pmod p,\qquad 
D\equiv -c\pmod p\qquad\bigl(c\in\mathbf Z\bigr). \tag{1}
\]

STEP 2.  (Using a double replacement to show \(p\mid c\)).  
Put  
\[
x_i=c+p s_i,\qquad D=-c+pD_1\qquad(s_i,D_1\in\mathbf Z).   \tag{2}
\]
Choose a two-element subset \(S=\{i,j\}\) and apply (A):
\[
p^{2}\mid D+x_i b^{\,k_i}+x_j b^{\,k_j}. \tag{3}
\]
Using (E) and (2) we expand the expression inside the divisibility in
(3) modulo \(p^{2}\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
D&+x_i b^{\,k_i}+x_j b^{\,k_j}\\
&\equiv (-c+pD_1)
        +(c+ps_i)(1+k_i p)
        +(c+ps_j)(1+k_j p)\pmod{p^{2}}\\
&\equiv\underbrace{\bigl(-c+c+c\bigr)}_{=\,c}
      +p\bigl(D_1+s_i+s_j+c(k_i+k_j)\bigr)\pmod{p^{2}}. \tag{4}
\end{aligned}
\]
Because (3) says that (4) is actually divisible by \(p^{2}\), its
constant term modulo \(p\) must vanish; hence
\[
c\equiv 0\pmod p. \tag{5}
\]
Thus \(p\mid c\).  Write  
\[
c=p t\qquad(t\in\mathbf Z). \tag{6}
\]

STEP 3.  (Renormalising the parameters).  
Substituting (6) into (2) gives
\[
x_i=p(t+s_i)=:p s_i',\qquad
D=p(D_1-t)=:pA \qquad\bigl(s_i',A\in\mathbf Z\bigr). \tag{7}
\]
Hence all \(x_i\) are already multiples of \(p\); we now prove that
they are multiples of \(p^{2}\).

STEP 4.  (Back to \(|S|=2\)).  
Rewrite (4) in terms of the new parameters in (7).
Because \(p\mid c\), the constant term has disappeared, and keeping
only terms up to \(p^{2}\) we obtain
\[
p\bigl(A+s_i'+s_j'\bigr)\equiv 0\pmod{p^{2}}
\Longrightarrow
A+s_i'+s_j'\equiv 0\pmod p\quad\text{for all }i\ne j. \tag{8}
\]

STEP 5.  (Using \(|S|=3\)).  
Choose three distinct indices \(i,j,k\) and invoke (A) for
\(S=\{i,j,k\}\):
\[
p^{3}\mid D+x_i b^{\,k_i}+x_j b^{\,k_j}+x_k b^{\,k_k}. \tag{9}
\]
Divide (9) by \(p\) (possible by (7)) and reduce modulo \(p^{2}\).
With \(x_\ell=p s_\ell'\) and (E) we get
\[
A+s_i'+s_j'+s_k'\equiv 0\pmod p. \tag{10}
\]

STEP 6.  (Solving the linear system).  
Subtract (8) from (10):
\[
\bigl(A+s_i'+s_j'+s_k'\bigr)-\bigl(A+s_i'+s_j'\bigr)\equiv s_k'\equiv
0\pmod p.
\]
Hence \(p\mid s_k'\) for every \(k\).  Write
\[
s_k'=p u_k,\qquad u_k\in\mathbf Z, \tag{11}
\]
and deduce from (7) that
\[
x_k=p^{2}u_k\qquad(1\le k\le n). \tag{12}
\]

Part II - proving \(p^{2}\mid y_i\).  
------------------------------------

STEP 7.  (The number \(E\)).  
Insert (12) into
\[
E=D+\sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k\,b^{\,k_k}.
\]
Because \(x_k\) already carries the factor \(p^{2}\) and
\(b^{\,k_k}\equiv 1+k_k p\pmod{p^{2}}\), every summand
\(x_k\,b^{\,k_k}\) is a multiple of \(p^{2}\).
Since \(D=pA\) with \(p\mid A\) by (8) and (11),
\[
E=p^{2}S\quad(S\in\mathbf Z). \tag{13}
\]

STEP 8.  (Condition (B)).  
Condition (B) states
\[
p^{4}\mid E+y_i\,b^{\,k_i}=p^{2}S+y_i\,b^{\,k_i}.
\]
Suppose \(y_i=p^{t}w\) with \(t=v_p(y_i)\) and \(p\nmid w\).
Using (E) we expand
\[
E+y_i\,b^{\,k_i}
=p^{2}S+p^{t}w\bigl(1+k_i p\bigr)+\text{ higher powers of }p.
\]

* If \(t=0\) the first term not divisible by \(p\) is
\(w\not\equiv 0\pmod p\); divisibility by \(p^{4}\) is impossible.  

* If \(t=1\) the least-power contribution is
\(p w\not\equiv 0\pmod{p^{2}}\); again impossible.  

Consequently \(t\ge 2\), i.e.
\[
p^{2}\mid y_i\qquad(1\le i\le n). \tag{14}
\]

Part III - conclusion.  
-----------------------

Combining (12) and (14) we have
\[
d_i-f_i=-(x_i+y_i)=-p^{2}\bigl(u_i+w_i\bigr),\qquad
u_i,w_i\in\mathbf Z,
\]
so
\[
p^{2}=11^{2}\ \mid\ (d_i-f_i)\quad\text{for every }i=1,2,\dots ,13.
\quad\square
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Higher-order replacements.  
   The problem demands divisibility not only for single-digit
   substitutions but for every simultaneous substitution of up to THREE
   digits, producing $\binom{13}{1}+\binom{13}{2}+\binom{13}{3}= 455$
   separate congruence conditions instead of the 13 conditions in the
   original and kernel versions.

2.  Non-trivial interaction of constraints.  
   The proof must show that the three-digit conditions force the common
   first-level difference c to be zero; with only the single-digit
   conditions c could be an arbitrary residue.  Detecting that the
   three-digit information kills the last degree of freedom is the main
   extra insight.

3.  Delicate use of the base.  
   Choosing base 12 together with modulus 11 (so that 12≡1) turns powers
   of the base into 1, drastically simplifying every positional weight
   and allowing the argument with triple substitutions to close.  The
   solver has to spot and exploit this arithmetic accident.

4.  Two-stage argument.  
   Even after the first chain (D→E) is mastered, one must restart the
   analysis for the second chain (E→F) and dovetail the two, observing
   that the vanishing of the first common difference forces E to be
   divisible by 11 and therefore annihilates the second common
   difference as well.

5.  Size and bookkeeping.  
   Keeping track of indices, subsets, and congruences across 13 digits,
   455 substituted numbers, and two successive replacement chains pushes
   the solver into heavier algebraic bookkeeping than either the
   original problem (9 digits, 9 conditions) or the former kernel
   variant (13 digits, 26 conditions).

These additional layers of combinatorial and arithmetic complexity make
the enhanced variant substantially harder and a genuine step up in
competition-level challenge.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
p:=11,\qquad b:=p+1=12,\qquad n:=p+2=13,\qquad 
k_i:=n-i\quad(1\le i\le n).
\]

For integers \(d_i,e_i,f_i\;(1\le i\le n)\) put  
\[
D=\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i\,b^{\,k_i},\qquad 
E=\sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i\,b^{\,k_i},\qquad 
F=\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i\,b^{\,k_i}.
\]

For every non-empty subset \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots ,n\}\) define the
single-step digit-replacement numbers  
\[
D[S\!\to\!E]\;:=\;
D+\sum_{i\in S}(e_i-d_i)\,b^{\,k_i},\qquad
E[i\!\to\!F]\;:=\;
E+(f_i-e_i)\,b^{\,k_i}\quad(1\le i\le n).
\]

Assume  

A)  For every non-empty \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots ,n\}\) with
   \(\lvert S\rvert\le 3\) one has  
   \[
      p^{\lvert S\rvert}\;\mid\; D[S\!\to\!E].
   \]

B)  For every index \(i\;(1\le i\le n)\) one has  
   \[
      p^{4}\;\mid\; E[i\!\to\!F].
   \]

Prove that, for every \(i\),  
\[
p^{2}\ \mid\ (d_i-f_i).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout the proof let \(v_p(\,\cdot\,)\) denote the
\(p\)-adic valuation.  
For every non-negative integer \(k\) we use the truncated binomial
expansion
\[
b^{k}=(1+p)^{k}=1+k\,p+\dfrac{k(k-1)}{2}\,p^{2}
             \pmod{p^{3}}. \tag{E}
\]

Introduce the differences  
\[
x_i:=e_i-d_i,\qquad y_i:=f_i-e_i\qquad(1\le i\le n).
\]

Part I - proving \(p^{2}\mid x_i\).  
-----------------------------------

STEP 1.  (Congruence modulo \(p\)).  
For the single-element set \(S=\{i\}\) condition (A) gives
\[
p\mid D+x_i b^{\,k_i}.
\]
Because \(b^{k_i}\equiv 1\pmod p\), we have
\[
D+x_i\equiv 0\pmod p\qquad(1\le i\le n).
\]
Hence the residues \(x_i\bmod p\) are all equal; write
\[
x_i\equiv c\pmod p,\qquad 
D\equiv -c\pmod p\qquad\bigl(c\in\mathbf Z\bigr). \tag{1}
\]

STEP 2.  (Using a double replacement to show \(p\mid c\)).  
Put  
\[
x_i=c+p s_i,\qquad D=-c+pD_1\qquad(s_i,D_1\in\mathbf Z).   \tag{2}
\]
Choose a two-element subset \(S=\{i,j\}\) and apply (A):
\[
p^{2}\mid D+x_i b^{\,k_i}+x_j b^{\,k_j}. \tag{3}
\]
Using (E) and (2) we expand the expression inside the divisibility in
(3) modulo \(p^{2}\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
D&+x_i b^{\,k_i}+x_j b^{\,k_j}\\
&\equiv (-c+pD_1)
        +(c+ps_i)(1+k_i p)
        +(c+ps_j)(1+k_j p)\pmod{p^{2}}\\
&\equiv\underbrace{\bigl(-c+c+c\bigr)}_{=\,c}
      +p\bigl(D_1+s_i+s_j+c(k_i+k_j)\bigr)\pmod{p^{2}}. \tag{4}
\end{aligned}
\]
Because (3) says that (4) is actually divisible by \(p^{2}\), its
constant term modulo \(p\) must vanish; hence
\[
c\equiv 0\pmod p. \tag{5}
\]
Thus \(p\mid c\).  Write  
\[
c=p t\qquad(t\in\mathbf Z). \tag{6}
\]

STEP 3.  (Renormalising the parameters).  
Substituting (6) into (2) gives
\[
x_i=p(t+s_i)=:p s_i',\qquad
D=p(D_1-t)=:pA \qquad\bigl(s_i',A\in\mathbf Z\bigr). \tag{7}
\]
Hence all \(x_i\) are already multiples of \(p\); we now prove that
they are multiples of \(p^{2}\).

STEP 4.  (Back to \(|S|=2\)).  
Rewrite (4) in terms of the new parameters in (7).
Because \(p\mid c\), the constant term has disappeared, and keeping
only terms up to \(p^{2}\) we obtain
\[
p\bigl(A+s_i'+s_j'\bigr)\equiv 0\pmod{p^{2}}
\Longrightarrow
A+s_i'+s_j'\equiv 0\pmod p\quad\text{for all }i\ne j. \tag{8}
\]

STEP 5.  (Using \(|S|=3\)).  
Choose three distinct indices \(i,j,k\) and invoke (A) for
\(S=\{i,j,k\}\):
\[
p^{3}\mid D+x_i b^{\,k_i}+x_j b^{\,k_j}+x_k b^{\,k_k}. \tag{9}
\]
Divide (9) by \(p\) (possible by (7)) and reduce modulo \(p^{2}\).
With \(x_\ell=p s_\ell'\) and (E) we get
\[
A+s_i'+s_j'+s_k'\equiv 0\pmod p. \tag{10}
\]

STEP 6.  (Solving the linear system).  
Subtract (8) from (10):
\[
\bigl(A+s_i'+s_j'+s_k'\bigr)-\bigl(A+s_i'+s_j'\bigr)\equiv s_k'\equiv
0\pmod p.
\]
Hence \(p\mid s_k'\) for every \(k\).  Write
\[
s_k'=p u_k,\qquad u_k\in\mathbf Z, \tag{11}
\]
and deduce from (7) that
\[
x_k=p^{2}u_k\qquad(1\le k\le n). \tag{12}
\]

Part II - proving \(p^{2}\mid y_i\).  
------------------------------------

STEP 7.  (The number \(E\)).  
Insert (12) into
\[
E=D+\sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k\,b^{\,k_k}.
\]
Because \(x_k\) already carries the factor \(p^{2}\) and
\(b^{\,k_k}\equiv 1+k_k p\pmod{p^{2}}\), every summand
\(x_k\,b^{\,k_k}\) is a multiple of \(p^{2}\).
Since \(D=pA\) with \(p\mid A\) by (8) and (11),
\[
E=p^{2}S\quad(S\in\mathbf Z). \tag{13}
\]

STEP 8.  (Condition (B)).  
Condition (B) states
\[
p^{4}\mid E+y_i\,b^{\,k_i}=p^{2}S+y_i\,b^{\,k_i}.
\]
Suppose \(y_i=p^{t}w\) with \(t=v_p(y_i)\) and \(p\nmid w\).
Using (E) we expand
\[
E+y_i\,b^{\,k_i}
=p^{2}S+p^{t}w\bigl(1+k_i p\bigr)+\text{ higher powers of }p.
\]

* If \(t=0\) the first term not divisible by \(p\) is
\(w\not\equiv 0\pmod p\); divisibility by \(p^{4}\) is impossible.  

* If \(t=1\) the least-power contribution is
\(p w\not\equiv 0\pmod{p^{2}}\); again impossible.  

Consequently \(t\ge 2\), i.e.
\[
p^{2}\mid y_i\qquad(1\le i\le n). \tag{14}
\]

Part III - conclusion.  
-----------------------

Combining (12) and (14) we have
\[
d_i-f_i=-(x_i+y_i)=-p^{2}\bigl(u_i+w_i\bigr),\qquad
u_i,w_i\in\mathbf Z,
\]
so
\[
p^{2}=11^{2}\ \mid\ (d_i-f_i)\quad\text{for every }i=1,2,\dots ,13.
\quad\square
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Higher-order replacements.  
   The problem demands divisibility not only for single-digit
   substitutions but for every simultaneous substitution of up to THREE
   digits, producing $\binom{13}{1}+\binom{13}{2}+\binom{13}{3}= 455$
   separate congruence conditions instead of the 13 conditions in the
   original and kernel versions.

2.  Non-trivial interaction of constraints.  
   The proof must show that the three-digit conditions force the common
   first-level difference c to be zero; with only the single-digit
   conditions c could be an arbitrary residue.  Detecting that the
   three-digit information kills the last degree of freedom is the main
   extra insight.

3.  Delicate use of the base.  
   Choosing base 12 together with modulus 11 (so that 12≡1) turns powers
   of the base into 1, drastically simplifying every positional weight
   and allowing the argument with triple substitutions to close.  The
   solver has to spot and exploit this arithmetic accident.

4.  Two-stage argument.  
   Even after the first chain (D→E) is mastered, one must restart the
   analysis for the second chain (E→F) and dovetail the two, observing
   that the vanishing of the first common difference forces E to be
   divisible by 11 and therefore annihilates the second common
   difference as well.

5.  Size and bookkeeping.  
   Keeping track of indices, subsets, and congruences across 13 digits,
   455 substituted numbers, and two successive replacement chains pushes
   the solver into heavier algebraic bookkeeping than either the
   original problem (9 digits, 9 conditions) or the former kernel
   variant (13 digits, 26 conditions).

These additional layers of combinatorial and arithmetic complexity make
the enhanced variant substantially harder and a genuine step up in
competition-level challenge.
